content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 29, 2007

Robin L. Clauson
City of Newport Beach
3300 Newport Blvd.
Newport Beach, California 92658-8915

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-07-033

Dear Mr. Clauson:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Newport Beach
City Councilmember Michael Henn regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because we do not have the necessary facts to make a
determination regarding some aspects of your question, we are treating your request as
one for informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS

1. May Councilmember Henn participate in decisions to adopt an ordinance that
may affect the location or operations of sober living businesses or residential recovery
treatment facilities in the city of Newport Beach (the "City")?

2. May Councilmember Henn serve on a committee that will review existing
laws, examine potential changes to city regulations and state law, and make
recommendations to the city council with regard to regulation of sober living group
homes that operate within the city?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Councilmember Henn may participate in decisions to adopt an ordinance that
may affect the location or operations of sober living businesses or residential recovery
treatment facilities in the City if it is not reasonably foreseeable (substantially likely) that

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-07-033
Page No. 2

the governmental decisions will have a material financial effect on any of his economic
interests.

2. Councilmember Henn may serve on a committee that may make
recommendations to the city council regarding regulation of sober living group homes
that operate within the City, so long it is not reasonably foreseeable that the committee's
recommendations regarding existing laws, potential changes to City regulations and state
law, would have a material financial effect on any of his economic interests.

FACTS

This letter is written on behalf of, and with the authorization from
Councilmember Henn, whose business provides general business advisory and consulting
services to various clients.

One of Mr. Henn's clients is R.E. Stangeland Enterprises, a sole proprietorship
operated by Mrs. Lilah Stangeland. Mrs. Stangeland is also the sole trustee of the Roger
and Lilah Stangeland Trust. Mr. Henn is compensated by R.E. Stangeland Enterprises.
In addition, the Trust owns approximately 55 percent of a holding company, which owns
and operates various subsidiary businesses, including two retail pharmacies located
within the City. During a March 26, 2007 telephone conversation with Mr. Henn, he
stated he is currently the director and chief financial officer of the holding company and
four subsidiary businesses owned by the holding company. He also stated he is in the
process of resigning from those positions in conjunction with a pending reorganization of
the businesses. Mr. Henn's benefits are not related to the financial performance of the
retail pharmacy businesses.

He receives no salary or other income from the holding company, or any of its
subsidiaries. However, he is provided medical coverage under the holding company's
health insurance policy. According to Councilmember Henn, his medical benefits from
the holding company will also be terminated within two to four weeks. None of these
business entities—the sole proprietorship, trust holding company or any subsidiary
business—is or is presently anticipated to be the subject of any decision before the City
council.

The City has established a Committee ("the Committee") made up of two council
members, two planning commissioners, and residents of the City to review existing laws
and examine potential changes to City regulations and state law with regard to regulation
of sober living group homes both licensed and unlicensed by the state that operate within
the City. In addition, in our March 19, 2007 telephone conversation, you stated that you
"did not see any reasonable impacts" on the arrangement between the group home clients
and the pharmacy businesses as the result of any pending decision before the city council.
The Committee's charge is to make recommendations to the city council for both changes
in state and local regulations. The pharmacies sell prescription drugs to the clients of two
of the sober living programs currently operating in the City by virtue of billing

File No. 1-07-033
Page No. 3

arrangements through those programs. The city council is considering adopting an
ordinance that will impose general regulations that may affect the location or operations
of sober living businesses or residential treatment facilities. The regulations would be
unrelated to the provision of services or the dispensing of medications to the clients of
those businesses.

Councilmember Henn wishes to know if he will have a disqualifying financial
interest in a future city council decision to adopt an ordinance affecting the location or
operations of sober living businesses or residential recovery treatment facilities. In
addition, he wishes to know if there are any constraints on his ability to serve on the
Committee, which will investigate and explore options but will not have any specific
decision before it.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the
prohibition in section 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides the
following eight-step analysis.

Steps One and Two: (1) Is the individual a "public official" and (2) is the
individual making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

The first two steps in the analysis are not at issue in your letter. As a city council
member, Mr. Henn is a "member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency" and, therefore, is a "public official" subject to the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act.³ (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a).) Additionally,
Councilmember Henn will be making, participating in making or influencing a
governmental decision if he makes recommendations to the city council or becomes
involved in decisions to adopt an ordinance that imposes general regulations affecting the
location or operations of sober living businesses or residential recovery treatment
facilities. (Regulations 18702.1 and 18702.2.)

³ Recusal Requirements: If a public official is enumerated in section 87200 (including city
council members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he
or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic
interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulatory
18702.3(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in the regulations 18702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

File No. I-07-033
Page No. 4

Step Three: What are the economic interests that might be a source of a
conflict of interest?

This step involves identifying the economic interests that may be impacted by a
governmental decision. Under section 87103 of the Act, there are six different ways of
identifying economic interests in order to determine if a governmental decision may
result in a conflict of interest for a public official. The economic interests pertinent to
your question are as follows:

1. Business Entity Investment, Employment or Management: An official has an
economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he
or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b)).

Parent-Subsidiary, Otherwise Related Business Entity: The Act also recognizes
that: "[a]n official has an economic interest in a business entity which is a parent or
subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business entity in which the official has one of
the interests defined in Government Code section 87103(a) or (d)."

Regulation 18703.1(d)(1) defines a parent-subsidiary relationship as follows:

"Parent-subsidiary. A parent-subsidiary relationship exists when one corporation
directly or indirectly owns shares possessing more than 50 percent of the voting power of
another corporation."

In your letter, you state that Councilmember Henn has a business that provides
general business advisory and consulting services. You also state that he is currently a
director and chief financial officer of the holding company and its subsidiary businesses.
We assume he has an investment of $2,000 or more in his consulting business. If so, he
has an economic interest in his business. In addition, since he is a director and CFO of
the holding company and its subsidiaries, he has an economic interest in the holding
company and its subsidiaries by virtue of his business position. (Section 87103(d).)

Furthermore, if the relationship between the holding company and its subsidiaries
meets the definition of parent-subsidiary set forth above, Councilmember Henn will also
have an economic interest in these entities, by virtue of the parent-subsidiary relationship
among the businesses.

Your facts indicate that in the near future Councilmember Henn will be resigning
as director of the holding company and its various subsidiaries. Once he resigns these positions,
Mr. Henn will cease to have a potentially disqualifying economic
interest in these entities due to his business position. However, he will continue to have
an economic interest in these businesses as source of income as discussed below.

File No. I-07-033
Page No. 5

2. Sources of Income: An official also has an economic interest in any person
from whom he or she has received income aggregating $500 within 12 months prior to
the time the relevant governmental decision is made. (Section 87103(c); Regulation
18703.3.)

R.E. Stangeland Enterprises/holding company: For purposes of this analysis, we
assume that Councilmember Henn has received $500 or more in income from
compensation paid to him by R.E. Stangeland Enterprises, and from the holding company
in the form of medical insurance payments.⁴ (Section 82030.) Therefore, both are
sources of income to him. Please note that income aggregating $500 or more within 12
months prior to the time a decision is made is disqualifying. (Section 87103(c).)

Holding Company Subsidiaries:

According to your facts, the holding company owns and operates various
subsidiary businesses, including two retail pharmacies located within the city. If the
relationship meets the definition of parent-subsidiary set forth above, the holding
company and in each of its subsidiaries—including the pharmacies—will be deemed
sources of income to Councilmember Henn.

Mrs. Lilah Stangeland:

Assuming that Councilmember Henn receives $500 or more in income from R.E.
Stangeland Enterprises, it is a source of income to him. In addition, R.E. Stangeland
Enterprises is solely owned by Mrs. Lilah Stangeland. Where a business entity is solely
owned or closely held, we have advised that the controlling business owner, in this case,
Mrs. Stangeland, is also a source of income to the official, and the official may not
participate in any decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on her. (Talley Advice Letter, No. A-96-204; Henischke Advice Letter, No. A-80-069; Kathe Advice Letter, No. I-91-507.)

Roger and Lilah Stangeland Trust:

Furthermore, the holding company is 55 percent owned by the Roger and Lilah
Stangeland Trust, of which Mrs. Stangeland is the sole trustee. With regard to disclosing
interests held in a trust, an individual has a direct, indirect or beneficial interest in a trust
pursuant to the Act if the individual is:

"(1) A trustor and:

"(A) Can revoke or terminate the trust;

⁴ Under Section 82030 "income" is defined as "a payment received, including but not limited to
any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, including any gift of food
or beverage, loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by the filer, reimbursement for
expenses, per diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any person other than an
employer, and including any community property interest in the income of a spouse."

File No. I-07-033
Page No. 6

"(B) Has retained or reserved any rights to the income or principal
of the trust or retained any reversionary or remainder interest; or

"(C) Has retained or reserved any power of appointment, including
but not limited to the power to change the trustee, or the power to
amend, alter or designate, either alone or in conjunction with
anyone else, the person or persons who shall possess or enjoy the
trust property or income.

"(2) A beneficiary and:

"(A) Presently receives income; or

"(B) Has an irrevocable future right to receive income or
principal . . .."

If Mrs. Stangeland has an interest in the trust pursuant to terms of Regulation
18234(c), then the assets of the Roger and Lilah Stangeland Trust, would be considered
as assets of Mrs. Stangeland as an individual. (Talley Advice Letter, No. A-96-204;
Silver Advice Letter, No. A-96-066; Irmas Advice Letter, A-92-199.) As noted above,
since Mrs. Stangeland is a source of income to Councilmember Henn, he may not
participate in any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on her.

3. Personal Finances: Councilmember Henn also has an economic interest in his
personal finances and those of his immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.) "A
governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing." (Ibid.) A reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a public official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-
month period.⁵ (Regulation 18705.5)

Your letter does not mention any impact on Councilmember Henn's personal
finances as the result of the upcoming decisions, but note that a conflict of interest may
arise if it reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material effect on his
personal finances.

⁵ Under regulation 18705.5, when determining whether a governmental decision has a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a financial
effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official, none a financial effect on the
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a
direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered.

File No. I-07-033
Page No. 7

Step Four: Are these economic interests directly involved in the
governmental decision?

Councilmember Henn's consulting business, R.E. Stangeland Enterprises, Lilah
Stangeland, the holding company, the pharmacies, and other subsidiary businesses:

A person, including business entities, sources of income and sources of gifts are
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when, directly or by an agent,
it:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person." (Regulation 18704.1(a).)

If a source of income is not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is
considered indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)

You stated in your letter that "[n]one of these business interests—the sole
proprietorship, trust holding company or any subsidiary business—is or is presently
anticipated to be the subject of any decision before the Newport Beach City Council." In
addition, you have not provided any facts that indicate that Councilmember Henn's
consulting business, R.E. Stangeland Enterprises, or Ms. Stangeland herself, would be
the subject of any decision before the city council.

Therefore, based on the facts presented, these sources of income are deemed
indirectly involved in the decisions.

Personal Finances:

If there is any financial effect on the personal finances of a public official or his or
her immediate family they are deemed to be directly involved in a decision. (Regulation
18704.5.) Because you have not presented any facts indicating a personal financial
effect, we do not analyze this factor.

Steps Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on
Councilmember Henn's economic interest be material, and reasonably foreseeable?

Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one of those economic interests by applying the applicable materiality

File No. I-07-033
Page No. 8

standard set forth in Commission regulations. (Regulations 18700(b)(5) and 18705, et
seq.) After finding the applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Regulation
18700(b)(6).)

For persons who are sources of income, the applicable materiality standard varies
depending on the financial size of the business entity in question.

Indirectly Involved Business Entities:

For business entities that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision,
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the materiality standards.

Subdivision (c)(4) of this regulation applies to small business entities, and if any
of the business entity economic interests identified above meet the following criteria in
this subdivision, the financial effects of the decisions will be "material":

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $20,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or,

"(c) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).)

Sources of Income who are individuals: 6

Under Regulation 18705.3, an effect of a decision is material as to an individual
who is a source of income to an official if any of the following applies:

"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments,
or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or

"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest
in a manner that is considered material under Title 2, California
Code of Regulations, sections 18705.2(b)."

The above materiality standard is applicable to Mrs. Lilah Stangeland, who is a
source of income to Councilmember Henn.

6 For sources of income that are business entities, the materiality standards are the same as those,
set forth above, for a general economic interest in a business entity.

File No. I-07-033
Page No. 9

Foreseeability:
Generally, the foreseeability analysis must be applied on a decision-by-decision
basis. Regulation 18706 provides: "A material financial effect on an economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, within the meaning of Government Code section 87103, if it is
substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards (see Regulations 18704
and 18705) applicable to that economic interest will be met as a result of the
governmental decision."

Without specific details regarding an upcoming decision, we cannot provide
guidance as to when it is reasonably foreseeable that the above materiality standards will
be met with regard to Councilmember Henn's various economic interests, including the
holding company and its subsidiaries, or the pharmacies, which sells prescription drugs to
clients of two sober living programs in the city.

For instance, if the decisions involving the ordinance impact the location or
operations of sober living facilities to the extent that it results in the termination of the
arrangement between the clients of the group homes and the pharmacies, or if the
decision will have a financial effect on the pharmacies of $20,000 or more, or affect the
assets of Mrs. Stangeland by $1,000 or more, then it is reasonably foreseeable that the
above materiality standards will be met.

However, the Commission cannot make the determination of foreseeability.
Ultimately, the determination of whether or not it is reasonably foreseeable that the
materiality standard will be met is a factual question for the councilmember to decide.

Personal Finances: An impact on an official's personal finances is considered to
be "material" if it amounts to $250 or more in any twelve-month period. (Regulation
18705.5.) You have not provided any information regarding potential impact on
councilmember Henn's personal finances, therefore we are unable to reach a
determination as to this issue.

Steps Seven and Eight: The "Public Generally" and "Legally Required
Participation" Exceptions

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public. (See Section 87103;
Regulations 18707-18707.10.)

The "legally required participation" rule applies when the official's participation
in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)

You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
Councilmember Henn's situation; therefore, we do not address them here. Without

File No. I-07-033
Page No. 10

further information regarding the specific decisions that may arise, we are unable to
render specific advice.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

may affect the location or operations of sober living businesses or residential recovery
treatrnent facilities in the city of Newport Beach (the,.City',)?
2. May Councilmember Henn serve on a cornmittee that will review existing
laws, examine potential changes to city regulations and state law, and make
recofilmendations to the city council with regard to regulation of sober living group
homes that operate within the citfl

may affect the location or operations of sober living businesses or risidential recovery
treatment facilities in the City if it is not reasonably foreseeable (substantially likely)ihat

the governmental decisions will have a material financial effect on any of his economic

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 March 29, 2007 Robin L. Clauson City of Newport Beach 3300 Newport Blvd. Newport Beach, California 92658-8915 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-07-033 Dear Mr. Clauson: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Newport Beach City Councilmember Michael Henn regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because we do not have the necessary facts to make a determination regarding some aspects of your question, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² QUESTIONS 1. May Councilmember Henn participate in decisions to adopt an ordinance that may affect the location or operations of sober living businesses or residential recovery treatment facilities in the city of Newport Beach (the "City")? 2. May Councilmember Henn serve on a committee that will review existing laws, examine potential changes to city regulations and state law, and make recommendations to the city council with regard to regulation of sober living group homes that operate within the city? CONCLUSIONS 1. Councilmember Henn may participate in decisions to adopt an ordinance that may affect the location or operations of sober living businesses or residential recovery treatment facilities in the City if it is not reasonably foreseeable (substantially likely) that ___________________________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.) File No. I-07-033 Page No. 2 the governmental decisions will have a material financial effect on any of his economic interests. 2. Councilmember Henn may serve on a committee that may make recommendations to the city council regarding regulation of sober living group homes that operate within the City, so long it is not reasonably foreseeable that the committee's recommendations regarding existing laws, potential changes to City regulations and state law, would have a material financial effect on any of his economic interests. FACTS This letter is written on behalf of, and with the authorization from Councilmember Henn, whose business provides general business advisory and consulting services to various clients. One of Mr. Henn's clients is R.E. Stangeland Enterprises, a sole proprietorship operated by Mrs. Lilah Stangeland. Mrs. Stangeland is also the sole trustee of the Roger and Lilah Stangeland Trust. Mr. Henn is compensated by R.E. Stangeland Enterprises. In addition, the Trust owns approximately 55 percent of a holding company, which owns and operates various subsidiary businesses, including two retail pharmacies located within the City. During a March 26, 2007 telephone conversation with Mr. Henn, he stated he is currently the director and chief financial officer of the holding company and four subsidiary businesses owned by the holding company. He also stated he is in the process of resigning from those positions in conjunction with
